Tomato_Bacterial_Spot.description: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves, stems, and fruit. Spots turn brown with yellow halos. Common in warm, wet weather.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide (e.g., Blitox 50) every 7–10 days
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves immediately
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at the base only
Do not work in the field when plants are wet
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds or resistant varieties
Rotate crops — avoid planting tomatoes in the same spot for 2 years
Maintain wide spacing between plants for air circulation
Apply a preventive copper spray before the monsoon season

Tomato_Early_Blight.description: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older, lower leaves. Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani.
treatment: Spray Mancozeb (0.25%) or Chlorothalonil every 7 days
Remove infected lower leaves and dispose away from the field
Apply organic neem oil spray (5ml/litre) as an early intervention
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertiliser — it encourages lush growth that blight loves
prevention: Mulch around plant base to prevent soil splash onto leaves
Water in the morning so leaves dry before evening
Use drip irrigation instead of sprinklers
Plant resistant varieties like Arka Vikas or PKM-1

Tomato_Late_Blight.description: Fast-spreading water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems, turning brown-black with a white mould on the underside in humid conditions. Can destroy a crop in days.
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) immediately at first sign
Spray Cymoxanil 8% + Mancozeb 64% every 5–7 days during active infection
Remove and burn all infected plant material — do NOT compost
Stop irrigation for 2–3 days to reduce humidity around plants
prevention: Avoid dense planting — keep at least 60cm between plants
Scout the field every 3 days during monsoon season
Apply preventive Bordeaux mixture (1%) at the start of rainy season
Never use infected plant debris as mulch

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.description: Pale green or yellow patches on the upper leaf surface with olive-brown velvety mould underneath. Thrives in high humidity and poor ventilation.
treatment: Spray Chlorothalonil 75% WP (0.2%) or Mancozeb every 10 days
Improve greenhouse/shade-net ventilation immediately
Remove badly infected leaves and discard away from the field
Reduce overhead watering completely
prevention: Maintain relative humidity below 85% in covered growing areas
Prune lower leaves to improve air flow
Avoid wetting foliage during irrigation
Use resistant varieties if growing under poly-house conditions

Tomato_Septoria_Leaf_Spot.description: Numerous small circular spots with dark brown borders and tan/grey centres, often with tiny black dots (pycnidia) inside. Starts on lower leaves and moves up.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP or Copper Oxychloride at first sign
Remove all infected leaves below the first fruit cluster
Spray every 7–10 days during wet conditions
Avoid handling plants when wet to prevent spread
prevention: Do not plant tomatoes in the same bed for at least 2 seasons
Keep the base of plants mulched to reduce soil splash
Stake plants upright to reduce leaf contact with soil
Use certified disease-free transplants

Tomato_Spider_Mites.description: Tiny mites cause fine yellow stippling on leaves. Heavy infestation leads to bronzing, webbing on undersides of leaves, and leaf drop. Worst in hot, dry conditions.
treatment: Spray Abamectin 1.8% EC (0.5ml/litre) or Spiromesifen
Wash plants with a strong jet of water to dislodge mites
Apply neem oil (3%) as an organic alternative
Rotate miticides — mites develop resistance quickly
prevention: Maintain adequate soil moisture — drought stress worsens mite outbreaks
Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes soft, mite-attractive growth
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) as biological control
Monitor the underside of leaves weekly

Tomato_Target_Spot.description: Brown lesions with concentric ring patterns on leaves, stems, and fruit. Similar to early blight but caused by Corynespora cassiicola. Affects all plant parts.
treatment: Apply Fluxapyroxad + Pyraclostrobin or Tebuconazole fungicide
Remove infected plant parts and destroy
Spray every 14 days during active disease pressure
Do not apply the same fungicide class more than twice in a season
prevention: Avoid planting in areas with poor drainage
Scout early — target spot spreads rapidly once established
Maintain good plant nutrition — stressed plants are more susceptible
Practise crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops

Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Leaves curl upward and inward, turn yellow, and plants become stunted. Caused by a virus spread by whiteflies. Infected plants cannot be cured — act fast.
treatment: There is NO cure — remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly vectors with Imidacloprid 17.8% SL (0.3ml/litre)
Apply yellow sticky traps throughout the field to catch whiteflies
Do not take cuttings or transplants from infected plants
prevention: Use whitefly-resistant or TYLCV-resistant varieties (e.g., Arka Samrat)
Install 40-mesh insect-proof netting in nurseries
Scout for whiteflies weekly and spray early
Maintain a 50-metre gap between new planting and old tomato fields

Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Leaves show mosaic pattern of light and dark green, become distorted or 'fern-like'. Fruit may be discoloured. Spread by contact, tools, and aphids.
treatment: No chemical cure — remove infected plants and destroy immediately
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution between plants
Control aphid vectors with Thiamethoxam 25% WG
Wash hands thoroughly after handling infected plants
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed
Do not smoke near tomato plants — tobacco can carry the virus
Control aphid populations before transplanting
Avoid wounding plants unnecessarily during cultivation

Tomato_healthy.description: No disease detected. Your tomato plant appears healthy. Keep up your current practices.
prevention: Continue regular scouting — check plants every 3–4 days
Maintain consistent watering and avoid water stress
Apply balanced fertiliser (NPK 19:19:19) every 3 weeks
Keep the field weed-free to reduce pest harborage
